market_context: The Feb 2-13 period was defined by three overlapping forces. First, the DeepSeek R1/V3 aftershock from late January continued to pressure AI capex narratives — the core question being whether US hyperscaler spending was misallocated if Chinese labs could train at a fraction of the cost. This created a bifurcation: infrastructure plays (AVGO, AMZN AWS, GOOGL Cloud) with hard contracted revenue held up better than pure GPU consumption stories. Second, a dense earnings calendar drove violent single-stock moves: PLTR beat massively on Feb 2 AH (61% guide raise) but the stock faded as the gain was already priced; AMD reported a Q4 beat on Feb 4 AH but guided Q1 to only $9.8B vs elevated AI hopes, triggering -17% on Feb 5 open; GOOGL reported Q4 Cloud +48% to $17.7B on Feb 4 AH, a strong beat, yet GOOGL drifted lower over subsequent weeks on tariff regime risk; AMZN reported AWS +24% on Feb 5 AH with record AWS margins. Third, tariff escalation intensified: China tariffs lifted toward 20%, semiconductor-specific tariff threats surfaced (25%+), and steel/aluminum tariffs were announced. A partial government shutdown delayed the January NFP to Feb 11; the actual print came in at +130K with unemployment steady at 4.3%, a 'Goldilocks' read but markets were already braced for something worse. AAPL faced accumulating pressure from App Store antitrust proceedings, China competition from Huawei, and supply chain tariff exposure despite record Q1 revenue announced Jan 29.

trades.thesis: Palantir reported Q4 2025 on Feb 2 AH: EPS $0.25 vs $0.23 est, revenue $1.41B vs $1.33B est (+70% YoY). FY2026 guide of $7.19B vs consensus $6.22B — a 61% YoY growth guide with massive upside. US commercial and government AI platform revenue inflecting. Buying the open Feb 3 to capture post-earnings momentum.
trades.catalyst: Massive Q4 beat + 61% FY2026 revenue guide raise vs consensus; AOS/AI Platform wins with government and enterprise clients accelerating
trades.lesson: Earnings beats with already-elevated sentiment can fade quickly even on spectacular guidance. PLTR had run 70%+ in prior 6 months — the guidance beat was priced into the pre-earnings price. Should have recognized the stock as 'good story, bad entry' rather than chasing post-print.

trades.thesis: AMD reports Q4 on Feb 4 AH. Beat on EPS/revenue likely but pattern risk: any guidance miss on Q1 vs AI-elevated expectations triggers institutional exit. AI GPU thesis for AMD has far less customer lock-in than NVDA — AMD guided Q1 to ~$9.8B, failing to match the market's bull-case hope of $11B+. High-volume pre-planned institutional selling anticipated from the open on Feb 5.
trades.catalyst: AMD Q4 AH results beat on actuals but Q1 guidance underwhelmed elevated AI expectations; $200M opex overshoot flagged by JPMorgan analyst; stock opened -17% Feb 5 — entered Feb 4 close to position for post-earnings gap down
trades.lesson: The -17% open on Feb 5 was the right call directionally, but AMD recovered significantly over 20 trading days as data center fundamentals held. Short thesis was correct on the immediate reaction but the 20-day window was too long — AMD's actual Q1 2026 Data Center revenue hit $5.8B (+57% YoY), causing a re-rate. Better exit would have been T+3 to T+5.

trades.thesis: Broadcom is the primary beneficiary of the hyperscaler custom ASIC trend (Google TPUs, Meta MTIA). Unlike NVDA which faces DeepSeek narrative risk, AVGO's AI revenue is tied to contracted ASIC roadmaps that don't get cancelled based on model efficiency stories. GOOGL Q4 Cloud +48% beat and $91.4B FY2025 capex confirm AVGO demand visibility. At $307 after the AMD-induced sector selloff, AVGO is compelling risk/reward.
trades.catalyst: GOOGL Q4 Cloud +48% beat on Feb 4 AH = direct AI capex tailwind for AVGO custom silicon; sector selloff created entry discount; AMZN AWS +24% beat on Feb 5 AH added further confirmation
trades.lesson: Custom silicon / contracted ASIC plays (AVGO, Marvell) are structurally more resilient to DeepSeek-style narrative shocks than GPU merchants. When the sector sells off on AI efficiency fears, the companies with hard committed ASIC revenue are the right buy. Conviction on differentiated thesis within sector dislocation is alpha.

trades.thesis: Three converging bear catalysts: (1) App Store antitrust class action trial scheduled Feb 2026, India threatening $38B fine, EU €500M fine already imposed — commission compression risk is structural. (2) Greater China competition from Huawei intensifying, evidence of iPhone share loss in the largest non-US segment. (3) Tariff exposure — AAPL assembly concentrated in China, incremental tariff cost estimated $800M+/year. Despite record Q1 revenue (Jan 29), these risks are not fully priced at $275+. Stock had run 16% in prior 3 months.
trades.catalyst: App Store antitrust proceedings imminent; tariff escalation on China imports; Huawei domestic competition in Greater China; Feb 13 article from Trefis flagging all three risks explicitly
trades.lesson: Multi-factor short thesis with regulatory + tariff + competitive pressure converging is powerful when a stock is near all-time highs following record earnings. The market eventually repriced AAPL lower as each risk factor incrementally materialized. Short against post-earnings euphoria with structural headwinds is high-conviction setup.

trades.thesis: Gold is the natural hedge in a trade-war-escalation regime. Tariffs on China (moving toward 20%), steel/aluminum tariffs announced, semiconductor tariff threats — all inflationary, all negative for risk assets, all positive for gold. Kevin Warsh Fed Chair nomination (hawkish) triggered anti-fiat trade repricing downward, but tariff inflation + uncertainty means gold benefits as real rate hedge and safe haven. GLD at $455 is below recent highs with clear regime support.
trades.catalyst: China tariff escalation toward 20%; steel/aluminum 25% tariff announcement; broader trade-war narrative driving safe-haven demand; inflation expectations rising from tariff pass-through
trades.lesson: In a tariff escalation regime, gold is a reliable long with positive carry from inflation expectations. The multi-week trade worked as anticipated. Safe-haven plays in uncertain macro regimes provide portfolio insurance AND positive P&L — ideal for a period like Feb 2026 where directional equity calls have wide dispersion.

trades.thesis: Amazon reported Q4 2025 on Feb 5 AH: net sales +14% to $213.4B, AWS revenue +24% to $35.58B (beat est $34.93B), AWS operating income $12.47B (beat $11.91B). Fastest AWS growth in 13 quarters. $200B FY2026 capex plan for AI infrastructure signals management conviction. AMZN provides direct access to the AI capex supercycle through AWS — more protected from DeepSeek model-efficiency concerns than GPU sellers because AWS revenue is compute/infrastructure, not model training-specific.
trades.catalyst: AWS +24% Q4 beat — fastest growth in 13 quarters; $200B FY2026 capex confirms AI infrastructure buildout; enterprise AI demand confirmed via earnings call
trades.lesson: Strong earnings catalyst was real but tariff regime headwinds capped the upside. AMZN is a global business exposed to trade friction — even with AWS strength, tariff/regulatory noise limited the 20-day gain to modest +1.5%. Should have sized AMZN smaller vs AVGO which had cleaner thesis.

trades.thesis: Super Micro Computer faces a deteriorating competitive position: (1) AI server demand is real but commoditizing — SMCI has no proprietary silicon moat, competing on rack integration against Dell, HPE. (2) SMCI has history of audit/accounting issues (2024 Hindenburg report, delayed filings). (3) Stock had risen 120%+ on AI euphoria, trading at elevated multiples for a contract manufacturer. (4) NVDA's H100/H200 allocation constraints have eased, reducing SMCI's ability to command premiums. AMD-induced chip selloff creates entry opportunity.
trades.catalyst: AI server commoditization thesis; SMCI accounting credibility overhang; premium valuations for a contract manufacturer not justified; post-AMD selloff sector pressure
trades.lesson: SMCI short worked well — the commoditization thesis and accounting overhang are structural negatives that market was still looking past. Short positions in 'picks and shovels' plays with no proprietary moat in AI bubble conditions is repeatable alpha. The stock continued declining well past the exit date.

trades.thesis: Post-DeepSeek clarity: the efficiency narrative does not eliminate GPU demand — Jevons paradox applies. Cheaper model training increases total AI application deployment, expanding GPU utilization. GOOGL $91.4B capex, AMZN $200B capex, META AI infrastructure — all confirmed commitments. NVDA Q4 FY2026 earnings expected Feb 26 with consensus expecting $20B+ revenue. At $190 post sector selloff, NVDA is 15% below Jan peak. Blackwell ramp should drive revenue acceleration.
trades.catalyst: Hyperscaler capex confirmations from GOOGL/AMZN/META in Q4 earnings; Jevons paradox reaffirming GPU demand post-DeepSeek; NVDA Q4 earnings catalyst Feb 26 expected to be blowout
trades.lesson: NVDA Q4 earnings (Feb 26) were a 'blowout that disappointed' — stock sank 5% despite strong numbers as investors were positioned for even more. The 20-day window ended before the post-earnings re-rate. The thesis was correct but timing was wrong — should have waited for the post-earnings dip (Feb 26-27) rather than entering Feb 9. Buying into a known catalyst 3 weeks out in a volatile name created unnecessary theta decay from elevated expectations.

trades.thesis: January NFP released Feb 11 (delayed from normal Feb 7 due to government shutdown). Consensus +70K. Multiple risk factors converging: (1) NFP delay itself signals government dysfunction — fiscal drag. (2) If number comes in weak (actual +130K but delayed, markets uncertain), risk-off. (3) Tariff regime = stagflation risk: inflation from tariffs + slowing job market = Fed trapped. (4) Tech valuations still elevated despite AMD -17%, SMCI weakness. Shorting QQQ as broad expression of tech/growth vulnerability to tariff-stagflation regime.
trades.catalyst: Delayed January NFP (government shutdown) creating uncertainty; tariff escalation = stagflation risk; tech valuations extended after QQQ hit 625+ in prior weeks; BofA calling selloff 'internally inconsistent' suggests contrarian pressure
trades.lesson: Macro shorts on broad indices work when the regime shift is real (tariff escalation) but the magnitude is capped by the market's willingness to buy dips. QQQ only fell ~2.5% over 20 days despite multiple negative catalysts — the market absorbed the pressure. Regime shorts need wider 20%+ downside to be worth the risk. Better to express regime risk through individual stock shorts (SMCI, AAPL) than broad index shorts.

trades.thesis: Alphabet reported Q4 2025 on Feb 4 AH: revenue +18% to $113.8B (beat $111.4B est), EPS $2.82 (beat $2.64 est, +31% YoY). Google Cloud +48% to $17.7B, a massive beat. Record operating cash flow $52.4B. This is a clean earnings beat with AI Cloud accelerating rapidly. At $331, GOOGL trades at reasonable multiple for a company with search dominance + fastest-growing cloud segment. Buying Feb 5 to capture post-earnings momentum.
trades.catalyst: Q4 2025 EPS +31% beat, Cloud +48%, record cash flow; Gemini AI integration driving enterprise adoption; cloud beat signals AI ROI realization for enterprise customers
trades.lesson: Worst trade of the period. GOOGL beat expectations by wide margins but the stock fell nearly 10% over 20 days driven by macro/tariff regime selling, broader tech de-rating, and possibly antitrust/regulatory concerns. This illustrates the key Feb 2026 lesson: strong earnings cannot overcome regime-level headwinds in a tariff escalation + macro uncertainty environment. Should have paired the GOOGL long with AAPL/AMD shorts to hedge the macro beta. Conviction in fundamentals without respecting the macro regime is a systematic error.

period_summary.regime_notes: Feb 2-13 2026 was a regime-transition period: the market moved from 'AI capex boom, all ships rise' to a more discriminating 'who has contracted revenue vs who has narrative exposure' framework. The DeepSeek efficiency shock (late Jan) separated AI infrastructure (AVGO, AMZN AWS) from GPU consumption plays (NVDA, AMD). Simultaneously, Trump tariff escalation created a dual headwind: (1) direct cost inflation for China-exposed companies (AAPL); (2) macro slowdown fears capping the upside for all risk assets. The period's alpha came from: SHORT against commoditized/overvalued AI stories (SMCI, AMD), SHORT against tariff victims (AAPL), and LONG on contracted AI infrastructure with hard committed hyperscaler capex (AVGO, GLD as hedge). The key mistake was buying GOOGL and PLTR post-earnings — strong beats in a macro headwind environment cannot overcome the regime discount. Going forward in this regime: favor shorts on companies with narrative-dependent valuations; favor longs with hard contracted revenue or safe-haven properties.
